nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing streams and smaller river systems with sandy or fine substrates, leaf litter, roots, and dense vegetation; found near river banks in the upper Araguaia basin in Goiás State

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — protects delicate barbels)
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for refuge
dense fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Taxiphyllum, Cabomba)
shaded hiding spots and low-light areas

diet.notes: The Sixray Corydoras is an omnivore that forages along the bottom, feeding in the wild on worms, small benthic crustaceans, insects, and plant matter. In the aquarium it readily accepts high-quality sinking micro-pellets, bottom-feeder wafers, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, Tubifex, and baby brine shrimp. Offering a varied diet is important for conditioning; this species should not be expected to survive on tank leftovers alone.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, green neon tetras)
small freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) in mature tanks
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that may intimidate or outcompete them
boisterous hyper-active bottom feeders that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Mike Noren; 1024x609. Two individuals together (ideal for this shoaling cory), front fish tack-sharp; clear marbled black-and-white body pattern and the diagnostic dark dorsal-fin blotch, barbels visible.
Mike Noren; 1024x685. Sharp single specimen resting on a plant leaf, full body in frame, marbled pattern and dark dorsal blotch clear; slight green cast.
Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric; 4032x2272 high-res. Sharp single fish on gravel showing full marbled pattern and barbels; green aquarium-light cast is the only caveat.

summary: The Sixray Corydoras (Aspidoras pauciradiatus), also sold as the False Corydoras, is a miniature catfish from the upper Araguaia basin in Brazil, reaching about 2.5 to 3.5 cm. It belongs to the genus Aspidoras rather than Corydoras, and is distinguished by having only six soft dorsal-fin rays. A group of six or more is required.
careNotes:
The Sixray Corydoras is one of the smallest catfishes available in the hobby, reaching only about 2.5–3.5 cm. It belongs to the genus Aspidoras, which is closely related to but distinct from Corydoras — the two groups differ in cranial structure, with Aspidoras lacking the nuchal plate (a bony shield on the back of the skull) found in true Corydoras. Despite the superficial resemblance that earns it the trade name 'False Corydoras', it should not be confused with similarly small cories such as Corydoras pygmaeus or C. habrosus. Among Aspidoras, this species is set apart by possessing only six soft dorsal-fin rays rather than the seven found in related species.

A mature, well-filtered aquarium of at least 54 L with a 60 × 30 cm footprint is the minimum, though a larger tank is preferable when housing a proper group of six or more. Fine sand substrate is essential — rough gravel will damage the sensitive barbels the fish use to probe for food. Adding leaf litter such as Indian almond leaves and providing twisted roots or driftwood replicates the natural stream environment and helps maintain the slightly acidic water chemistry this species tends to prefer.

Water quality must be excellent. Regular partial water changes are important because the species is sensitive to accumulated nitrates and declining water quality. Temperature tolerance is broader than many tropical catfishes — 20–27°C — making it suitable for cooler tropical or subtropical community setups. It tends to do well alongside White Cloud Mountain minnows or other fish that share the lower end of this temperature range.

Like all members of the family Callichthyidae, the Sixray Corydoras is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dash to the surface to gulp air. This behaviour is entirely normal, and the tank should have a secure lid to prevent escape. The pectoral-fin spines are hardened and can pierce skin; a plastic container or bag is safer than a net when moving these fish. The spines also carry a mild venom gland, so handling with care is advised.

Keep in groups of at least six, with eight to ten being strongly preferred, to encourage the natural active foraging behaviour the species displays in the wild. Specimens kept in smaller numbers tend to hide and are rarely seen.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Sixray Corydoras is considered difficult and requires deliberate preparation. Begin by conditioning the group over several weeks with a varied diet of live and frozen foods to bring the fish into peak condition. Spawning is typically triggered by a large partial water change of 50–75% using cooler, very soft, and slightly acidic water — mimicking the onset of a tropical rainy season. Target water conditions of around pH 6.0 and GH 0–1 dH for the change. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or Java moss and receive no parental care, so adults should be removed promptly after spawning to prevent them from eating the eggs. Eggs generally hatch in approximately 3–5 days depending on temperature. Raise the fry first on infusoria and vinegar eels, then graduate to micro-worms and newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow. A dedicated breeding tank is strongly recommended.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aspidoras pauciradiatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (30–35 mm), temperature (20–27°C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), minimum tank footprint (60 × 30 cm / ~54 L), native range (upper Araguaia basin, Goiás State, Brazil), breeding trigger (large cool water change), and diet (omnivore forager accepting sinking dried, live, and frozen foods).

additional.name: FishBase — Aspidoras pauciradiatus (species #12190)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), maximum size (2.9 cm SL), temperature (22–25°C), pH (6.0–7.2), native range (upper Araguaia basin, Brazil), benthopelagic habit, schooling behaviour, and facultative air-breathing. FishBase max size of 2.9 cm SL is standard length; total length used in profile (~3.5 cm) per SeriouslyFish and trade observations.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Aspidoras pauciradiatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 22–25°C, pH 6.5–7.0, GH 8–12), adult size (3–3.5 cm), peaceful schooling behaviour, sandy substrate requirement, breeding notes (acid soft water, eggs among fine-leaved plants, fry on vinegar eels).

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics — Sixray Cory Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided tank size guidance (minimum 38–75 L / 10–20 gal), compatibility recommendations (small tetras, rasboras, pencilfish, dwarf cichlids), substrate advice (fine sand essential), diet guidance (sinking pellets, frozen foods), and care level characterisation (moderate / intermediate).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sixray corydoras
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names (sixray corydoras, false corydoras), taxonomy, native range (upper Araguaia River basin, Brazil), diet (worms, benthic crustaceans, insects, plant matter), egg-laying without parental care, and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning is typically triggered by a large cool water change into very soft, acidic conditions (pH around 6.0, GH 0–1 dH). Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants and receive no parental care; adults should be removed after spawning. Fry are raised on infusoria, then progressed to vinegar eels and baby brine shrimp. A dedicated breeding tank is strongly recommended.
